does: This paper develops a concrete framework for constructing safety cases based on chain-of-thought (CoT) monitoring in advanced reasoning models. The core contribution is a two-part safety case architecture: first, establishing that models lack dangerous capabilities without their CoT, and second, ensuring that any dangerous capabilities enabled by CoT are detectable through monitoring. The authors systematically identify and categorize threats to CoT monitorability—linguistic drift, steganography, and alien reasoning—analyze their potential drivers, and evaluate both existing and novel techniques for maintaining CoT faithfulness. They further investigate methods for extracting interpretable, monitorable reasoning from non-monitorable CoT outputs, providing a structured approach to addressing the problem of uninterpretable intermediate reasoning in safety-critical deployments.
connects: This work builds on decades of interpretability research in neural networks and extends recent advances in chain-of-thought prompting by reframing interpretability as a safety problem rather than merely an explainability one. It connects to broader control and trustworthiness safety case literature, which has traditionally relied on capability limitations. The paper responds to the challenge posed by scaling laws and emergent capabilities by proposing monitoring-based safety cases as a complement to inability-based arguments. It engages with mechanistic interpretability work on understanding neural computation while acknowledging the practical challenge that reasoning might be encoded in ways humans cannot easily detect, drawing parallels to concerns about goal misalignment in advanced AI systems.
enables: This roadmap enables future work on monitoring-based safety governance for reasoning models approaching human-level and superhuman capabilities. By identifying specific threats to monitorability—steganography and alien reasoning—it clarifies what technical problems must be solved before CoT monitoring can provide genuine safety assurance. The framework points toward developing better faithfulness evaluation metrics, more robust techniques for extracting interpretable reasoning, and methods for certifying that a CoT is genuinely revealing a model's actual reasoning process. Success in this agenda could support deployment of advanced reasoning systems with greater confidence in their safety properties, while failure cases would motivate exploring alternative safety approaches, thus advancing the broader research program of making AI systems safe at dangerous capability levels.
